data.title: Minecraft Creator Calls Using AI To Write Code 'An Incredibly Bad Idea' — Says Advocates Are 'Incompetent Or Evil'
data.description:
Artificial intelligence coding tools are gaining traction in software development despite warnings from Minecraft creator Markus Persson.

"Reminder that using AI to write code is an incredibly bad idea still, and anyone advocating for it is either incompetent or evil," he wrote on X in late January.

"It's just as dumb as letting AI write the laws," Persson added. "It's about logic, not about typing."

Earlier that month, he expanded on his concerns. "If you seriously believe that the same people that manipulate people via mainstream media (for clearly evil purposes) wouldn't use subtle ways to use AI to manipulate us, you're dumb," he said. "It's being forced down our throats for a reason. Don't rely on AI for programming."

Vibe Coding Gains Momentum

AI-assisted coding continues to expand. AI developer Anthropic hosted a global hackathon last month with roughly 13,000 participants.

AI researcher Andrej Karpathy wrote on X around the same time that programming is becoming "unrecognizable" as AI systems increasingly generate and refine code.

Trending: A single bad hire can set a startup back years. Here are the 5 hires founders most often misjudge — and why

Security And Skill Concerns

Questions about oversight and reliability have surfaced.

For Persson, however, the broader concern centers on skill.

That experience shapes his message to developers entering an AI-driven industry.

"Learn. Your. Craft. You are not safe to program if you don't know how to do it," Persson wrote on X in January. "It's like only knowing how to fly, but not how to land or take off."

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: 'You're Going To Be Fine,' Dave Ramsey Tells 47-Year-Old Couple With $200K Income — Worried They Need $2.7M To Retire
data.description:
A seven-figure retirement target can feel overwhelming, even for high earners.

Angie reached out to "The Ramsey Show" from San Francisco with a concern weighing on her and her husband. The 47-year-old couple earns about $200,000 a year and carries no debt beyond their mortgage. Online calculators projected they may need as much as $2.7 million to retire.

"You're going to be fine," personal finance expert Dave Ramsey said. 

A Late Start Meets A Strong Income

Angie said she earned minimum wage for most of her career and began making significantly more about five years ago. She and her husband began saving 15% of their income for retirement after listening to "The Ramsey Show" for about a year.

Ramsey focused on that annual contribution. He said saving that amount for 15 to 17 years would amount to "a lot of money." Once their house is paid off next year, they can increase their savings even further.

Trending: Most founders obsess over the wrong hires. See the 5 startup roles that actually determine whether a company scales or stalls.

The Math Behind The Target

Ramsey said retirement calculators use assumptions about inflation and withdrawal rates that can drive the projected total higher. He then laid out his approach.

The stock market has averaged about 11% over time, he said. He plans around 12% returns in mutual funds and uses 4% as a long-term inflation estimate based on decades of consumer price index data.

Using those assumptions, a $1 million portfolio earning 12% would generate $120,000 in a year, according to Ramsey's projections. Leaving 4% in the account would maintain purchasing power, while the remaining 8% could be withdrawn as income without reducing principal.

"So if you want to live on 200,000 a year at that time then you're going to need two million dollars," he said.

Why Long-Term Projections Change

Ramsey said long-term calculations rarely match the exact outcome. He said income contributions often rise over time and that inflation and rates of return do not move exactly as projected. 

Many people end up wealthier than their calculators initially suggest, he said. They stay invested and engaged.

Co-host George Kamel said he enjoys using investing calculators. "It's incredible," he said. "I love using the investing calculator and start dreaming." 

Image: Shutterstock
